draft.introduction: Ecclesiastes (Qoheleth) opens with what may be the most pessimistic sentence in the entire biblical canon: “Vanity of vanities, all is vanity.” The Hebrew hevel (הֶבֶל) means “breath,” “vapour,” “emptiness”—something that appears substantial but dissolves at a touch. The Preacher surveys the whole of human endeavour and pronounces it meaningless. Nothing is new. Nothing lasts. The sun rises and sets; the rivers flow to the sea; the generations come and go; and nothing changes. This is the anti-theosis of despair. Where the mystics promise ascent to the divine, Qoheleth sees only the wheel. Where the prophets promise transformation, Qoheleth sees repetition. There is no progress, no transcendence, no escape from the cycle. The text does not deny God’s existence, but it denies that God’s existence makes any practical difference to the human condition. The human being is trapped under the sun, and nothing done under the sun endures. If theosis means rising above the human condition to participate in the eternal, Qoheleth’s answer is that there is no “above.”

translation.text: The words of the Preacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem. Vanity of vanities, says the Preacher, vanity of vanities—all is vanity. What does a man gain by all the toil at which he toils under the sun?
translation.original_text: דִּבְרֵי קֹהֶלֶת בֶּן־דָּוִד מֶלֶךְ בִּירוּשָׁלָיִם הֲבֵל הֲבָלִים אָמַר קֹהֶלֶת הֲבֵל הֲבָלִים הַכֹּל הָבֶל

translation.text: A generation goes and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever. The sun rises and the sun goes down, and hastens to the place where it rises. The wind blows to the south and goes around to the north; around and around goes the wind, and on its circuits the wind returns. All streams run to the sea, but the sea is not full; to the place where the streams flow, there they flow again.
translation.original_text: דּוֹר הֹלֵךְ וְדוֹר בָּא וְלְעוֹלָם הָאָרֶץ עֹמָדֶת

translation.text: All things are wearisome, more than one can say. The eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the ear filled with hearing. What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done, and there is nothing new under the sun. Is there a thing of which it is said, “See, this is new”? It has been already in the ages before us. There is no remembrance of former things, nor will there be any remembrance of later things yet to be among those who come after.
translation.original_text: כָּל־הַדְּבָרִים יְגֵעִים לֹא־יוּכַל אִישׁ לְדַבֵּר מַה־שֶּׁהָיָה הוּא שֶׁיִּהְיֶה

translator_notes.note: The Hebrew hevel (הֶבֶל) is notoriously difficult to translate. “Vanity” (KJV) suggests moral emptiness; “meaninglessness” (NIV) suggests nihilism; “absurdity” (Fox) suggests existentialism. The literal meaning is “breath” or “vapour”—something insubstantial that appears and vanishes. The superlative havel havalim (“vanity of vanities”) follows the Hebrew pattern of “song of songs” or “king of kings”—it means the ultimate, the absolute instance of the category.

The cosmic imagery of sun, wind, and water creates a picture of eternal recurrence without progress. This is not the Greek philosophical concept of cyclical time (as in the Stoic ekpyrosis) but something bleaker: pointless repetition. The streams run to the sea but the sea is never full—the image suggests effort without accumulation. Against the theosis traditions that promise progressive ascent toward the divine, Qoheleth offers a universe that goes nowhere.
